dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit M177 for a reported cardiac arrest in [CITY].
Hi, I'm EMT Ryan. Fire has already started CPR; let's confirm she's pulseless and apneic.
Fire reports the patient has no breathing or pulse and has been in cardiac arrest for approximately nine minutes.
She is unresponsive-no eye opening, verbal response, or motor activity.
GCS is 3: eyes 1, verbal 1, motor 1. She gives no response to any stimuli.
Fire has inserted a King airway. Placement confirmed by mist, breath sounds, and capnography.
No breathing detected. Respiratory rate is zero and breath sounds are absent.
No palpable pulse. Confirming blood pressure and skin condition now.
Blood pressure is 0/0. She is warm to touch with no signs of bleeding.
Bilateral breath sounds are absent. Airway remains secure.
Copious gastric emesis was noted after airway insertion. No distention observed.
No visible signs of rectal bleeding. Color of urine unknown; no void noted.
She remains warm to the touch. No deformities or wounds noted.
Has a history of primary hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Current meds unknown.
Family reported she was last seen alive 30 minutes ago. No details about last oral intake.
Fire found her unresponsive on the floor with no breathing or pulse.
No pulse detected. Continuing compressions.
Respiratory rate is zero. BVM ventilations ongoing.
Blood pressure is 0/0. No circulation noted.
SpO₂ is zero. Pulse oximeter remains undetectable.
Blood glucose is 377 mg/dl. Consistent with type 2 diabetes.
Initial EKG failed due to monitor issue. Confirmed PEA rhythm on second monitor.
IO access established in left tibia. Confirmed with positive marrow return.
Administered first dose of epinephrine 1 mg IO. Normal saline flush followed.
Multiple 4-lead ECG attempts failed. Monitor issues resolved, rhythm remains PEA.
Activating cardiac arrest protocol. Continue compressions, ventilation, and medications.
Still no pulse. CPR ongoing.
No respirations. BVM ongoing via King airway.
EKG remains PEA. Let's give the next dose of epinephrine.
Delivered second dose of epinephrine 1 mg IO with normal saline flush.
Administered 250 mL normal saline bolus through IO line.
Pain assessment is not applicable. She is unresponsive with GCS 3.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME], the nearest trauma center for this cardiac arrest.
She is secured supine on the stretcher with all restraints applied for safe transport.
Due to her large size, we used a Reeves stretcher for movement out of the living room.
Nasogastric tube initiated en route and suction is ongoing. Airway remains secure.
Weight and location made patient secure difficult; but transport has been completed without incident.
Patient remained unresponsive with no pulse or breathing the entire transport time.
[CITY], this is EMS2. Assisting with transport and monitoring en route.
En route with 50-year-old female in cardiac arrest-PEA confirmed, epinephrine 1 mg IO x 2, King airway in place, NG suction active, and secure.
No known allergies reported or found on patient.
Unable to obtain current medications; fire reported no meds administered to patient on scene.
Skin is warm, intact, and dry. No signs of petechiae, rashes, or bruising.
No bleeding from trauma, wounds, or airway noted on secondary survey.
Rechecked rhythm. PEA persists since initial assessment.
IO line functional and used for medications and fluids. Humoral IO could not be established due to patient size.
Patient was moved via low friction draw sheet method to the hospital bed upon arrival.
Delivered patient to [COMPANY_NAME] Trauma Room 1. Handing over to hospital staff.
Attempted third epinephrine IO dose. No improvement noted.
Glucose remained elevated at 377 mg/dl throughout the event.
No incident during loading or movement; restraints held and mobility maintained.
Continue epinephrine as scheduled, call ED en route with updates as needed.
Rhythm unchanged en route. Adjusted compressions when possible during Gastric decompression.
Family last saw her 30 minutes ago. Found patient unresponsive between bed and wall.
No blunt trauma or penetrating injury noted representing clear cause of arrest.
No neck trauma, no signs of head injury, and no agitation or posturing found.
Continue BVM and assess tube every few minutes. Airway remains clear and functional.
Ventilation occurring with minimal resistance, chest not rising; but King tube remains placed.
EKG unchanged with PEA noted in and out of ambulance.
Lost connection for a brief time during run; re-established via second monitor.
Airway maintained at all times, even during movement. No dislodgement incidents.
Fire reported patient found in bed area. No prior collapse or witnessed events.
No attempts at Advanced Airway due to patient stability and secured airway by Fire.
[CITY] selected for strongest cardiac crash care and closest after assessment.
Gastric contents drained during transport via NG suction.Patient stable for continued BVM.
Airway secured and no air leak noted; continue monitoring on arrival.
ED- this is EMS2, have another dose of Epi ready upon arrival if not given en route.
[CITY]- patient arrived Hot. Remaining GCS3, no pulse, PEA rhythm. No changes during run.
Used depth markers during insertion procedures to avoid overinsertion and airway trauma.
No need for bagging during transport due to continuous compressions and BVM assisted flow.
Family was with us en route. Provided updates in clear terms but patient outcome doubtful.
No additional interventions planned per protocol until ED arrival. Protocol followed in entirety.
Two providers managed the move from ambulance to ED bed using draw sheet technique.
[CITY]-repeat IO access attempts were unsuccessful on second site due to anatomical landmarks lost to size again.
Final dose of Epi given at 18:15. No response observed, subsequent not attempted.
Two units at scene- Fire and EMT with EMS2. All aware. Critical care team inbound.
Patient moved without resistance using draw sheet; shorten lift time, lower healthy provider strains.
No back strain felt despite the task; lift planned with four-hand technique followed & supported by USA.
IO line still patent and board functioning for fluids and meds in attached.
Blood glucose levels unchanged, and the contamination was cleared with confirmed reading.
Patient met with trauma team and room correctly. Handover reported by fire as complete.
Safe and sound from stretcher to bed-patient in supine position consistent with scene and transport needs.
No complications from movement. PI or concussion not suspected on or after transport.
Chips-Also note that NG tube ongoing for suction-possibly could be adjusted or evaluated at x-ray.
Provided the full list of procedures performed in the field, including PEA monitoring and medicine administration.
Epi documented for 18:10 and 18:15. Time intervals tracking correctly with protocol.
Scene moved quickly, appropriate time given and protected for family view and emotional surge.
No re-checked EKG performed in ambulance due the consistent negative trend.
Fast and direct to the nearest trauma center. Other hospital bypassed for primary management.
No delay tactic taken, just direct to [CITY] for clear designation and more specialist care.
No other AED used due to unresponsive rhythm and focus on PEA with meds- confirmed by three EMTs.
All care continuous during transport with breaks <90 seconds, as per best CPR practices, without compromise.
Attempted to make transportation smooth and support team for more strong mechanical compressions.
Included information to hospital on stroke care- no CT prior- they to consider based on needs.
This one to be scanned, evaluated again and prepared for x-ray-also following documentation may be utilized.
No rapid sequence attempts or secondary protocol used- just PEA strategy.
All tools and devices maintained through run; equipment hold long enough to complete transport.
Time of call and transport documented and followed- no delays except for initial monitor 'down time'.
For more documentation and hospital transfer forms, let us prepare and give or transfer to EMT team.
Wrapped up EMT part of call. All actions taken as per protocol. Readiness for hospital own.
